schools.summary: Ellicott: the time is 'purposely indefinite,' the story absent from Chronicles, and the phrase 'sought the face of the LORD' hints at a separate source.
Henry: God's judgments look far back; Saul's violation of the sworn oath made his a 'bloody house,' and David rightly enquired into the cause of the famine.
Wesley and Clarke: the events belong long before Absalom, out of order; Saul was punished in life, yet God may justly visit a king's guilt on his people and posterity.
Gill: no such massacre of the Gibeonites is recorded in Saul's own history, though it is here reckoned for.

debates.question: When did the famine and expiation take place?

sides.position: long before Absalom's rebellion, recorded here out of chronological order
the time is purposely indefinite, after Mephibosheth was known and likely before Absalom

debates.question: Why should the land and Saul's sons suffer for Saul's crime, so long after?

sides.position: God justly visits a king's guilt on his people and posterity; the people were likely complicit
the delay teaches that guilt is not worn out by time; Providence gives no account of its reasons

themes: the broken oath to the Gibeonites

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil-Delitzsch: 'Amorites' is a general name for the Canaanite Hivites; Israel sinned and was punished as a nation, so all Israel suffered until the oath-breach was purged.
Wesley: Saul 'sought' to wear the Gibeonites out under colour of justice, oppressing them with excessive labours.
Gill: David sent messengers to summon the Gibeonites to hear the wrong done them.

debates.question: Why was Saul's sin punished so long after, and on all Israel?

sides.position: Israel sinned and was punished as a nation; Saul acted as king, involving all Israel in the broken oath
the delay was heightened by Saul's many other sins that occupied his lifetime's judgment

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'make atonement' is the Law's technical word, to 'cover up' Saul's sin from the Gibeonites and from God, that His blessing might return.
Clarke objects that it is 'very strange' the terms of atonement should be left to such a people rather than sought of God himself.
Gill and Keil-Delitzsch read the opening of the negotiation: David seeks satisfaction for the injury so the Gibeonites may bless the LORD's inheritance.
Henry: in vain do we expect mercy from God unless we do justice upon our sins; just restitution retrieves the blessing.

debates.question: Should the choice of atonement have been left to the Gibeonites?

sides.position: it is strange and improper; the matter should have been asked of God himself
David rightly seeks to cover the sin before both the earthly sufferers and God

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Gibeonites refuse money (forbidden by Numbers 35:31) and disclaim any random Israelite's blood, asking only from the offender's house.
Henry: money is no satisfaction for blood; the Gibeonites forbore even their lawful claim to freedom, keeping the covenant they were not bound to keep.
Gill notes only the turn of the dialogue, 'in reply to his motion.'

themes: blood cannot be bought with money

schools.summary: Gill: 'consumed' covers both Saul's cruel oppressions and his outright slaughter of the Gibeonites.
Keil-Delitzsch: David could grant the demand because Numbers 35:33 requires blood-guiltiness on the land to be expiated by the criminal's blood.
Henry: the sin 'devised' against a people waits to be reckoned for, falling on the man who consumed them and his house.
Ellicott: 'seven' marks completeness; 'unto the LORD' means publicly, not a human sacrifice; Saul sinned as God's chosen ruler and must be punished before the LORD.
Clarke doubts the seven were guilty; Wesley defends the act as done by divine command, since God was well pleased with it.
Gill: the number seven likely matched the sons available besides the spared Mephibosheth.

debates.question: Was the hanging of the seven a just execution or an unwarranted human sacrifice?

sides.position: a just, public, representative punishment done by God's command and approval
there is no proof the seven were guilty participants in Saul's crime

themes: 'unto the LORD' as public, not sacrifice

schools.summary: Keil-Delitzsch: David spared Mephibosheth for the LORD's oath with Jonathan; Ellicott adds that sparing the sole male heir clears David of self-interest.
Wesley: the Gibeonites asked only a number, so the choice of whom to spare lay with David.
Gill notes the careful naming that distinguishes this Mephibosheth from Rizpah's son of the same name.
Henry: avenging one broken oath, David took care not to break another sworn to Jonathan.
Ellicott: 'Michal' is a scribal error for Merab, who bore Adriel's sons; David's choice of victims disproves any self-strengthening motive.
Clarke: Michal was never Adriel's wife and was childless; Kennicott MSS and the Chaldee read Merab, which 'cuts the knot.'
Henry: the giving of Merab to Adriel to provoke David is now quietly reckoned; injury to God's faithful ones meets a severe, if late, retribution.
Gill identifies Rizpah as Saul's concubine.

debates.question: Does the text's 'Michal' name Michal or Merab?

sides.position: a scribal error for Merab, who alone married Adriel and bore children

schools.summary: Ellicott: the execution at barley harvest (mid-April, after Passover) set Rizpah's watch at about six months, until the autumn rains proved divine forgiveness.
Clarke dates barley harvest to the vernal equinox, about the 21st of March.
Gill keeps the count of seven straight: two sons of Rizpah and five of Merab, fallen together.
Henry: they were sacrificed at harvest to turn away the wrath that had withheld harvest-mercies and to obtain favour for the present crop.

themes: judgment at the hinge of the grain-year

schools.summary: Henry: Rizpah's watch is both excessive grief and noble love; she submits to the sentence yet shows her sons died for their father's sin, not their own.
Clarke protests that God requires no human blood for sacrifice and the famine might have been purged by lawful sacrifice; Wesley reads her sackcloth-tent and silent testimony of her sons' innocence.
Gill: the sackcloth served both as mourning and as a shelter against the weather through the long watch.

debates.question: Was this expiation something God truly required?

sides.position: God does not require man's blood for sacrifice; the land could have been purged by lawful sacrifice and humiliation
the execution was the appointed satisfaction of justice, after which vengeance from heaven ceased

debates.question: What is the 'water dropped from heaven' that ended the watch?

sides.position: the fixed autumnal rains about October, so the watch lasted over five months
a rain sent perhaps not many days after, as the sign that God was entreated

themes: the sign of rain from heaven

schools.summary: Gill: the report moved David to pity and to an honourable burial, showing the deed was done in obedience to God, not private revenge.
Keil-Delitzsch: the news is the bridge to David's gathering of the whole fallen royal house into Saul's family grave.
Wesley: David so approved Rizpah's piety that he imitated it, doing the honour he had neglected, to comfort the disconsolate widow.

themes: a woman's fidelity moves the king

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil-Delitzsch reconcile 'wall of Beth-shan' with 'the street' as the open square inside the gate; the notice opens the seam to the war-records.
Clarke recalls that the men of Jabesh burned the bodies and buried the bones under a tree, which David might have dug up again.
Gill notes the distance from Jerusalem and suggests David acted by his messengers.
Henry: let not royal dust be lost among common graves; humanity obliges respect for the bodies of the great and good.

themes: the wall vs the street reconciled

schools.summary: Gill: the bones had lain under an oak at Jabesh, where the loyal men of that city had buried them.
Wesley: they are called bones because the flesh remaining was first burnt off, as with the men of Jabesh.
Henry: once God's anger was turned away, the hanged were no longer to be looked on as a curse.

themes: two companies of the dead joined
piety of Jabesh and of David meeting

schools.summary: Ellicott: Zelah is a town of Benjamin (Joshua 18:28), probably near Gibeah, the fit site of Saul's family grave in the sepulchre of Kish.
Wesley: 'after that' means after the hanging, for by that God was pacified, not by the burial.
Gill: the hanged were buried together with Saul and Jonathan in one family tomb.
Henry: when justice is done on earth, vengeance from heaven ceases, and God was entreated for the land.

themes: burial in the sepulchre of Kish
expiation by the execution, not the burial

schools.summary: Henry: aged David goes down in person ('he grows old, but not indolent'), waxes faint, is rescued by God's providence; even strong saints faint yet stand and are made more than conquerors.
Ellicott and Keil-Delitzsch: the war-records, drawn from a chronicle of David's wars, are set here as proofs of deliverance; 1 Chronicles 20 places them only by juxtaposition.
Clarke notes the war and David's fainting are found nowhere else in Scripture.
Gill: the Philistines were emboldened by their giants and by the decline of David's life.

themes: the fainting saint who stands his ground

schools.summary: Ellicott floats the emendation 'they halted at Gob'; Keil-Delitzsch keep Ishbi-benob as a proper name, a descendant of Raphah the father of the Rephaim.
Clarke: 'sword' is not in the original, so the 'new' thing is new armour or an unknown weapon.
Gill fixes only the giant's pedigree, of the race of the giants.

debates.question: Is 'Ishbi-benob' a personal name or a corrupted phrase?

sides.position: a genuine proper name meaning 'his dwelling is on the height'
a corruption best emended to 'they halted at Gob'

themes: David within a spear's length of death

schools.summary: Clarke: David is the lamp guiding all Israel; he cites Homer's Iliad to show how natural the metaphor of the leader as his people's light.
Ellicott: the grammar leaves it doubtful whether Abishai or David slew the giant; the title 'light of Israel' implies David's rule was already long established.
Henry: valuable lives ought to be doubly preserved; the men who could not keep David from Absalom now restrain him from the Philistines.
Gill: Abishai, seeing David's danger, made haste to his relief.

debates.question: Who slew the Philistine, Abishai or David?

sides.position: Abishai succoured and smote him
Abishai rescued, but David himself struck the killing blow

schools.summary: Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott handle the Gob/Gezer/Gath variants and identify Sibbechai (Mebunnai) the Hushathite, captain of the eighth division, and Saph (Sippai).
Clarke reports the manuscript reading 'Nob' for 'Gob' in many codices.
Gill sets the sequence: after this, after the former battle.
Henry: the giants fell by the hand of David's servants, for God chooses the weak to confound the mighty.
Clarke: a manifest corruption; Chronicles shows Elhanan slew Lahmi the brother of Goliath, the error arising from 'oregim' and 'Beth-hallachmi.'
Ellicott agrees the text is corrupt and restored from Chronicles; Keil-Delitzsch report the dispute over which text is original, preserving the Jewish tradition that Elhanan was David.
Gill marks it only as another battle in the same place.

debates.question: Whom did Elhanan slay, and is the text corrupt?

sides.position: a copyist's error; Chronicles is original, and he slew Goliath's brother Lahmi
the priority of the two texts is disputed, though the sense is Goliath's brother

schools.summary: Keil-Delitzsch treat the six fingers and toes soberly, citing Pliny's sedigiti, and parse the giant's title as 'a man of measures.'
Clarke matches the polydactyly with modern cases, then unleashes twelve objections charging the whole chapter with scribal corruption, yet ends confessing God's foundation stands sure.
Gill harmonizes quietly: another battle, another of the race of the giants.

debates.question: How far is the text of this chapter to be trusted?

sides.position: much of the chapter is a scribal ruin of glosses and corruptions
the difficulties are explicable details of a genuine record

outliers.position: Clarke treats the bulk of the chapter as textually corrupt and its facts distorted

themes: the foundation of God stands sure

schools.summary: Ellicott: Jonathan was David's nephew of a clan of heroes; Keil-Delitzsch explain the summary's grammar and insist 'by the hand of David' means David personally fought Ishbi-benob.
Gill: the giant defied Israel as Goliath had done years before.
Henry: David began his glory with one giant and ends with four; it is folly to glory in strength, for God confounds the mighty by the weak.

themes: by David's hand and his servants'

schools.summary: Gill: the four were of the race of the giants, descendants of the Anakim left in Gath after Joshua's conquest, with rabbinic lore on 'Repha' and Orpah.
Ellicott: all were descendants of Rapha but not necessarily sons of one man, of the Rephaim stock of Gath.
Henry: the most powerful enemies are reserved for the last conflict; death is a son of Anak, yet through Christ believers are more than conquerors.
